setting.note: The whole novel is packed into Les Halles, the great iron-and-glass food market that fed Paris for eight hundred years, and the tangle of old streets around it under the church of Saint-Eustache. Zola makes the market a character, "some colossal belly, bolted and riveted, built up of wood and glass and iron": the fish and vegetable and butter pavilions at dawn, the Quenu-Gradelle pork shop on the Rue Rambuteau, the crooked Rue Pirouette. The escaped political prisoner Florent, gaunt and half-starved, washes up in this world of plenty. The pavilions were demolished in 1971; that absence is what the historical layer is for.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Emile Zola, first published 1873; the author died in 1902.
rights.translation: English translation by Ernest Alfred Vizetelly; he died in 1922, so the translation is public domain in the US and UK/EU.
rights.territoryNotes: Public domain in the US and UK/EU: Zola died 1902, Vizetelly 1922.
rights.attribution: Emile Zola, The Belly of Paris (1873), trans. E. A. Vizetelly. Text from Project Gutenberg.

palette.accentSourceQuote: the deep-toned green of the foliage
palette.note: The sea of vegetables banked up against the pavilions at dawn, the book's central image of abundance. A deep market green that clears WCAG AA on paper.
